title: Did YouTube Just Kill AI Voiceovers?

transcript.text: All right, if you are making faceless YouTube shorts, you're probably using an AI voice over. There's a lot of tools for this, but the most popular one has always been 11 Labs. It's so common that if you scroll on the YouTube shorts feed for like 2 minutes, you'll encounter probably hundreds of AI voice overs. I even have my own voice cloned with AI, which a lot of my community uses. So, if my voice sounds familiar, that's probably why. That's called influence. But, in a recent update, YouTube actually announced that they're partnering with 11 Labs to tag all of their voice overs. So, in this video, I want to talk about what that means for the future, if it's a big deal or not, if you should be worried about it, and what you can do about it. Now, before I say anything, I do want to warn you guys to be careful with any type of information that you're consuming from YouTube updates. There's just so much misinformation in this space. Just a year ago, on like July 15th, everybody thought that faceless YouTube shorts were dead because of another policy update, and I had to make another video just like this one, and it's on this channel to this day. So, I'm just going to give you guys the raw info on what to do about it. There's not going to be any fear mongering. Make sure you watch to the end so I can clear everything up for you guys. I want to get this out of the way right now because I know some people won't stay till the end. You do not need to stop using AI voices, and no, they are not dead. There's only one thing that I would recommend going forward, and it's really just a precautionary measure. So, let me dive into the actual update so you can see what happened. So, I think a lot of people are worried because of this Twitter post. And again, guys, there's just so much misinformation going on. As you can see, this got 120,000 views. But, basically, it's this screenshot that just says, "At the presentation, Google announced that 11 Labs, along with OpenAI, are officially adopting Google's SynthID technology. Instead of trying to fight each other, these major AI companies are forming a unified front. By joining this initiative, 11 Labs will now embed Google's imperceptible digital watermarks directly into the AI audio voices and music they generate. Because 11 Labs is adopting it, Google's ecosystem, like Chrome and Google Search, will be able to seamlessly detect and flag if an audio clip or voice over was originally created using 11 Labs tools. That's it. That's all the screenshot said. And somehow, from that, everybody got that 11 Labs voice overs are going to be demonetized or banned. That's just not what this means. To some extent, there was already some detection of AI voice overs and really any AI tools in YouTube's back end before this. But, now people think that something massive is going to change. It's just not. Really, you have to think about this from a business perspective. Like Eleven Labs is not going to partner with YouTube if it's going to completely kill their business model. If YouTube just demonetized or banned AI voices overnight, Eleven Labs would be like a massive opt to YouTube because that's where most of their use comes from. So, in fact, this partnership is actually a great thing. It means that YouTube is actually seeing a future where Eleven Labs is part of this huge creator ecosystem. And if anything, the companies that haven't partnered with Google, AI content that doesn't have Synth ID or is less detectable will be more risky because there's that potential that will be there to mismatch what is actually disclosed versus what is actually in the video. And if nothing else calms you down, it should be this, you know, YouTube and Google, they are a business, Eleven Labs is a business, and they will not come to work together if it's detrimental to their pockets in any way. Now, as far as I understand it, this massive push to actually detect AI voices or partner with Eleven Labs is really mostly due to this EU AI Act, which you guys might have heard about, and I'll literally show you in the law what they're trying to do. If you scroll down, you'll see the section that says transparency risk. And what they say directly is, "This refers to the risk associated with the need for transparency around the use of AI." The AI Act introduces specific disclosure obligations to ensure that humans are informed when necessary to preserve trust. Moreover, providers of generative AI have to ensure that AI-generated content is identifiable. And guys, this isn't even necessarily talking about, you know, your fruit videos or like random 3D slop animations. They list what they mean. Like, on top of that, certain AI-generated should be clearly and visibly labeled, namely, literally by name, deepfakes and text published with the purpose to inform the public on matters of public interest. So, YouTube and other social media platforms are just trying to comply with this, mostly when it comes to like politics, when people are spreading fake news or pretending that something actually happened when it didn't happen. And the issue is when they don't disclose that, most people have like two brain cells and can't understand that this is actually AI slop. So, that's the whole thing. It does not really apply to you who's just making faceless YouTube shorts. It's not something to be worried about, but there is one change that I'm going to make personally going forward into the future just to make sure that I'm 100% safe, which is when I'm uploading, if I use AI voice overs from this point on, I'm probably going to check the altered content box, this yes right here. I usually press no here because yeah, it doesn't really apply to the content that I'm making cuz it even says what they're really trying to avoid, which is like making a real person appear or say to do something they didn't say or do, which is like deep fakes, alters footage of a real event or place, again, just like fooling people, it can be used to trick people, and generates a realistic looking scene that didn't actually occur. To follow YouTube's policy, you're required to tell us if your content is altered or synthetic and seems real. So, like, is it really a big deal when you're using a voice clone? No, but just to be safe, I'm going to click this if I use AI voice overs just because it says this includes realistic sounds or visuals made with AI or other tools. And this doesn't do anything. It's not going to reduce your reach or anything like that, and you probably do not need to do this. Like I said, I'm just being super safe. I like to run my business as risk averse as possible, so that's what I'll be doing and probably what I'll recommend to people. And again, you know, we keep in mind here, examples of altered or synthetic content. This is directly from Google. This is directly from YouTube. Under examples of content creators don't have to disclose, one of those things that's listed as minor is cloning one's own voice to create voice over or dubs. Obviously, this is not an exhaustive list, but you really don't need to even click the altered content box. I'm just probably going to do it going forward just so that there's not a mismatch between what is actually in my video and what is not because a lot of times when people get demonetized or banned, it's because of this mismatch where their video is AI, but they're not disclosing it. They're not getting demonetized or banned because the video is AI. They're getting demonetized and banned because they're not disclosing it. And now that YouTube has the ability to detect and flag these things even stronger and harder than before, I suspect that will become more important. But the fear mongering is not a big deal. You don't need to stop using AI voices, and we're not all cooked. I hope this video helps some of you guys out and ease your nerves. If you're interested in making YouTube shorts your full-time income, you can apply to work down below directly with me. I take people, work with them, and help them monetize their first channel or scale up their existing channels. You get direct access to me and all the resources that I have that I wish I had when I was starting so that you don't have to be guessing anymore. So, if you're interested in learning directly from me how I make $10,000 a month with faceless YouTube shorts, you can apply in the first link in the description. Also, I'm launching my software very soon, so stay tuned for that cuz if you're a faceless YouTube shorts creator, this is going to cook. I hope this video helps some of you guys out and peace out.
